sources.sourceName: Introduction to Sociology, OpenStax 3e (Pressbooks adaptation) - Appendix: Chapter Quiz Answers (odd-numbered)

meta.notes: Sociology (PSBB) items scraped verbatim from OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e Ch. 20 and Ch. 21 section-quiz pages; correct answers from the OpenStax-derived odd-numbered answer key (Pressbooks CC BY 4.0 adaptation). Live OpenStax page shows CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 plus a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing/non-AI per PRD.

hints.prompt: This item asks for the approximate WORLD population in 1999. World population is measured in what order of magnitude?
hints.rationale: World population is measured in billions of people.

hints.prompt: Which listed figure is clearly too high to be the world population in 1999, being closer to a far-future projection?
hints.rationale: Ten billion far exceeds the 1999 population and reflects a future projection.

hints.prompt: Which two options are the two HIGHEST figures listed, and thus overshoot a 1999 population that had just doubled to a single-digit-billions total?
hints.rationale: Seven billion and ten billion are the two highest figures, overshooting the 1999 total.

questions.stem: The population of the planet doubled in fifty years to reach _______ in 1999?
questions.explanation: The world population reached approximately 6 billion in 1999, having roughly doubled over the preceding fifty years.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 20 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 20 Q1 = A.

hints.prompt: 'Carrying capacity' comes from ecology and concerns how much an ENVIRONMENT can support before running out of resources. It is about supporting what?
hints.rationale: Carrying capacity concerns how many living things an environment can sustainably support.

hints.prompt: 'Carrying capacity' is about SUSTAINABILITY of life in an environment, not literal physical WEIGHT. Which distractor mistakenly reads 'carrying' as bearing physical weight?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The amount of weight urban centers can bear if vertical growth is mandated

choices.label: B
choices.text: The amount of life supported sustainably in an environment

hints.rationale: The 'weight urban centers can bear' option misreads 'carrying' as literal physical weight.

hints.prompt: Which two options are unrelated to how much life an environment can sustain, one being about welcoming immigrants and the other about cultural globalism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'The ability of a community to welcome new immigrants' and 'The capacity for globalism within a given ethnic group'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'The amount of life that can be supported sustainably' and 'The weight urban centers can bear'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'The amount of weight urban centers can bear' and 'The ability to welcome new immigrants'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'The capacity for globalism within an ethnic group' and 'The amount of life supported sustainably'

hints.rationale: Welcoming immigrants and cultural globalism have nothing to do with how much life an environment can sustainably support.

questions.stem: What does carrying capacity refer to?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The ability of a community to welcome new immigrants

choices.label: B
choices.text: The capacity for globalism within a given ethnic group

choices.label: C
choices.text: The amount of life that can be supported sustainably in a particular environment

choices.label: D
choices.text: The amount of weight that urban centers can bear if vertical growth is mandated

questions.explanation: Carrying capacity is the amount of life (e.g., population) that an environment can support sustainably given its resources.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 20 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 20 Q3 = C.

hints.prompt: A 'cornucopia' is a horn of plenty symbolizing abundance, so 'cornucopian' theory is OPTIMISTIC about handling population growth. What is the source of that optimism?
hints.rationale: Cornucopian theory is optimistic that human ingenuity and innovation will handle population growth.

hints.prompt: Cornucopian optimism rests on HUMAN action (ingenuity), not on nature acting on its own. Which distractor instead credits NATURE providing automatically?

choices.label: A
choices.text: That the earth will naturally provide enough for whatever number of humans exist

choices.label: B
choices.text: That human ingenuity will solve any issues

choices.label: C
choices.text: That new diseases keep populations stable

choices.label: D
choices.text: That population reduction is the real risk

hints.rationale: The 'earth naturally provides' option credits nature automatically, not human ingenuity.

hints.prompt: Cornucopian theory is optimistic that human ingenuity handles GROWTH. Which two options are the poorest fit, one relying on disease as a natural check and the other claiming the real danger is population DECLINE?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'That new diseases will always keep populations stable' and 'That the greatest risk is population reduction, not population growth'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'That human ingenuity will solve any issues overpopulation creates' and 'That the earth will naturally provide enough'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'That the earth will naturally provide enough' and 'That new diseases will keep populations stable'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'That the greatest risk is population reduction' and 'That human ingenuity will solve any issues'

hints.rationale: Relying on disease as a check or claiming decline is the real danger both contradict cornucopian optimism about ingenuity handling growth.

choices.label: A
choices.text: That human ingenuity will solve any issues that overpopulation creates

choices.label: B
choices.text: That new diseases will always keep populations stable

choices.label: C
choices.text: That the earth will naturally provide enough for whatever number of humans exist

choices.label: D
choices.text: That the greatest risk is population reduction, not population growth

questions.explanation: Cornucopian theory holds that human ingenuity and technological innovation will solve the problems created by population growth.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 20 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 20 Q5 = A.

hints.prompt: For a permanent CITY to exist, people must produce enough surplus food and organize together. This means a city depends on more than one kind of prerequisite, including what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A supportive environment, a food surplus, and social organization

choices.label: B
choices.text: Only good weather and nothing else

hints.rationale: Cities depend on multiple prerequisites, including environment, food surplus, and social organization.

hints.prompt: Which of the following is one genuine prerequisite for a city to exist?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Advanced agricultural technology to produce a food surplus

choices.label: C
choices.text: No form of social organization at all

hints.rationale: Advanced agriculture producing a food surplus is a real prerequisite for supporting a city.

hints.prompt: You have confirmed that an agricultural surplus is a real prerequisite for a city. Before choosing only that, what should you verify?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Whether a supportive environment and social organization are ALSO required

choices.label: D
choices.text: Nothing; one real prerequisite is always the full answer

hints.rationale: Since a city requires several prerequisites together, check the other options before settling on one.

questions.stem: What are the prerequisites for the existence of a city?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Good environment with water and a favorable climate

questions.explanation: Cities require a supportive environment, agricultural surplus, and strong social organization, so all of the above are prerequisites.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 20 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 20 Q7 = D.

hints.prompt: 'Exurbs' are communities located BEYOND the suburbs, even farther from the city center. They form when people seek to move ______ the crowded center.
hints.rationale: Exurbs form as people move farther away from the crowded urban center.

hints.prompt: The stem attributes exurbs to URBAN SPRAWL and crowding pushing people outward. Which distractor instead blames a specific real-estate event?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Urban sprawl and crowds moving into the city

hints.rationale: 'The housing boom of the 1980s' names a specific real-estate event, not the sprawl and crowding the stem emphasizes.

hints.prompt: Exurbs arise from sprawl and crowding pushing people outward. Which two options describe different, specific processes rather than that outward push?

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'Urban sprawl and crowds moving into the city' and 'The high cost of suburban living'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'The high cost of suburban living' and 'The housing boom of the 1980s'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'Gentrification' and 'Urban sprawl and crowds moving into the city'

hints.rationale: A specific decade's housing boom and inner-city gentrification are distinct processes, not the sprawl-driven outward push that creates exurbs.

questions.stem: What led to the creation of the exurbs?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Urban sprawl and crowds moving into the city

choices.label: B
choices.text: The high cost of suburban living

questions.explanation: Exurbs developed as urban sprawl and crowding pushed people to settle in communities beyond the suburbs.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 20 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 20 Q9 = A.

hints.prompt: 'Gentrification' is when wealthier people and investment move into a lower-income urban neighborhood, raising property values and rents. A direct effect of rising rents on existing lower-income residents is that they may be ______.
hints.rationale: Rising rents from gentrification tend to price out and displace lower-income residents.

hints.prompt: Is 'the city becomes more crowded' the defining effect of gentrification the item emphasizes?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No; gentrification is defined more by displacement of lower-income residents than by raw crowding

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, crowding is its defining feature

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, it always empties the city

hints.rationale: Gentrification is characterized by displacement of lower-income residents rather than simple crowding.

hints.prompt: Which two options can be eliminated, one because crowding is not gentrification's defining effect and the other because it depends on every listed effect being true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'They become more crowded' and 'All of the above'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'Less affluent residents are pushed into less desirable areas' and 'Traffic issues, including pollution, become worse'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'Traffic issues, including pollution, become worse' and 'They become more crowded'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'All of the above' and 'Less affluent residents are pushed into less desirable areas'

hints.rationale: Crowding is not the defining effect, and 'all of the above' depends on every listed effect holding, so both are weak.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Less affluent residents are pushed into less desirable areas.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Traffic issues, including pollution, become worse.

questions.explanation: Gentrification raises property values and rents, pushing less affluent residents out of newly desirable neighborhoods into less desirable areas.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 20 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 20 Q11 = B.

hints.prompt: The sociological study of 'urbanization' examines cities from many angles. This means it is a ______ field.
hints.rationale: The study of urbanization is broad and multi-faceted.

hints.prompt: Which of the following is a legitimate angle within the sociological study of urbanization?
hints.rationale: Urban politics is a legitimate topic within the study of urbanization.

hints.prompt: You have confirmed that urban politics is studied under urbanization. Before choosing only that, what should you verify?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Whether urban economics and urban environments are ALSO studied

choices.label: C
choices.text: Whether the option has fewer letters

choices.label: D
choices.text: Nothing; one true topic is always the complete answer

hints.rationale: Since urbanization spans several topics, check the other options before settling on one.

questions.stem: Urbanization includes the sociological study of what?
questions.explanation: The sociological study of urbanization spans urban economics, politics, and environments, so all of the above applies.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 20 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 20 Q13 = D.

hints.prompt: Human activity can harm the water supply through various forms of ______.
hints.rationale: Human activity harms water mainly through pollution and contamination.

hints.prompt: Which of the following is a real way human activity contaminates water?
hints.rationale: Creating sewage is a real way humans contaminate the water supply.

hints.prompt: You have confirmed that creating sewage harms the water supply. Before choosing only that, what should you verify?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Whether spreading chemicals and increasing radioactivity ALSO harm water

choices.label: B
choices.text: Whether 'sewage' happens to be option A

choices.label: C
choices.text: Whether the option is the shortest

choices.label: D
choices.text: Nothing; one true impact is always the full answer

hints.rationale: If several activities each harm water, a single-impact choice may be incomplete, so check the others.

questions.stem: What are ways that human activity impacts the water supply?
questions.explanation: Human activity affects the water supply through sewage, chemical pollution, and radioactive contamination, so all of the above applies.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 20 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 20 Q15 = D.

hints.prompt: Shoreline dredging disturbs the seafloor and coastal waters, so its harms fall mainly on what?
hints.rationale: Dredging mainly harms marine and coastal ecosystems.

hints.prompt: Damaged coral reefs, dead marine life, and ruined sea grass beds share what feature that 'reduction of human population' does not?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They are all direct harms to marine ecosystems

choices.label: B
choices.text: They are all about human demographics

choices.label: D
choices.text: They are all unrelated to water

hints.rationale: The first three are direct harms to marine ecosystems, unlike a change in human population.

hints.prompt: This is a 'not' question. Which two options ARE clearly negative marine outcomes of dredging, and so are not the exception?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'Damaged coral reefs' and 'Death of marine life'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'Reduction of human population' and 'Damaged coral reefs'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'Ruined sea grass beds' and 'Reduction of human population'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'Death of marine life' and 'Reduction of human population'

hints.rationale: Damaged coral reefs and dead marine life are direct negative marine outcomes of dredging.

questions.stem: What is not a negative outcome of shoreline dredging?
questions.explanation: Shoreline dredging damages coral reefs, kills marine life, and ruins sea grass beds; it does not reduce the human population.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 20 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 20 Q17 = D.

hints.prompt: 'E-waste' is discarded electronics. The item raises an environmental-justice point about where rich nations' e-waste ends up. Environmental injustice often means hazards are shifted onto whom?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Poorer, less powerful nations or communities

hints.rationale: Environmental injustice typically shifts hazards onto poorer, less powerful nations or communities.

hints.prompt: 'Peripheral' nations, in world-systems terms, are which?
hints.rationale: Peripheral nations are lower-income, less powerful nations, which is where much e-waste is sent.

hints.prompt: The item makes an environmental-justice point about e-waste being sent to poorer nations. Which two options are the poorest fit, one being an optimistic 'reused in new devices' outcome and the other a claim about ocean dumping?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'Repurposed into new electronics' and 'Dumped into ocean repositories'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'Recycled in peripheral nations' and 'Repurposed into new electronics'

hints.rationale: Being reused in new devices or dumped in the ocean does not fit the point about e-waste being exported to poorer nations.

questions.stem: Where does a large percentage of e-waste wind up?
questions.explanation: A large share of electronic waste is shipped to and 'recycled' in peripheral (low-income) nations, often under hazardous conditions, an example of environmental injustice.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 20 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 20 Q19 = B.

hints.prompt: A 'social movement' is best defined as what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Organized collective action aimed at social or political change

hints.rationale: A social movement is organized collective action aimed at social or political change.

hints.prompt: The Tea Party, Greenpeace, and the NAACP share what goal that defines them as social movements?
hints.rationale: The Tea Party, Greenpeace, and NAACP all pursue social or political change, unlike a sports league.

hints.prompt: This is a 'not' question. Which two options ARE clearly social movements, and so are not the exception?
hints.rationale: Greenpeace pursues environmental change and the NAACP pursues civil-rights change, both social movements.

questions.stem: Which of the following organizations is not an example of a social movement?
questions.explanation: A social movement is organized collective action aimed at social or political change. The NFL is a sports league, not a social movement; the Tea Party, Greenpeace, and NAACP are.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 21 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 21 Q1 = A.

hints.prompt: 'Collective behavior' refers to relatively SPONTANEOUS, loosely organized action by a gathering of people. Which feature best characterizes it?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A relatively spontaneous gathering, not a tightly structured group

hints.rationale: Collective behavior is a relatively spontaneous gathering rather than a tightly structured group.

hints.prompt: A class field trip and a soldier following a chain of command are TIGHTLY structured settings. How do they differ from collective behavior?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They are formally organized, unlike the loose gathering of collective behavior

choices.label: B
choices.text: They are more spontaneous than a crowd

choices.label: D
choices.text: They are identical to a spontaneous crowd

hints.rationale: A class trip and a military order are formally organized, unlike the loose gathering of collective behavior.

hints.prompt: Collective behavior involves a loosely gathered crowd. Which two options are the poorest fit, one being a formally organized class activity and the other just two individuals?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'A class going on a field trip' and 'Going shopping with a friend'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'A group of people interested in hearing an author speak' and 'A soldier questioning orders'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'A soldier questioning orders' and 'A class going on a field trip'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'Going shopping with a friend' and 'A group of people interested in hearing an author speak'

hints.rationale: A class field trip is a formally organized activity and shopping with a friend involves only two people, neither of which is a loosely gathered crowd.

questions.stem: Which of the following is an example of collective behavior?

choices.label: B
choices.text: A group of people interested in hearing an author speak

choices.label: C
choices.text: A class going on a field trip

questions.explanation: Collective behavior involves a relatively spontaneous gathering of people, such as a crowd assembled to hear an author speak, rather than tightly structured groups or individual acts.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 21 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 21 Q3 = B.

hints.prompt: Emergent-norm theory views crowds as developing NEW NORMS as members interact. This implies the theory sees crowd members as basically what?
hints.rationale: Emergent-norm theory sees crowd members as rational people who develop shared understandings.

hints.prompt: Older 'contagion' views portrayed crowds as irrational and impulsive. How does emergent-norm theory DIFFER from that older view?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It sees crowds as developing their own reasonable norms, not as inherently irrational

choices.label: D
choices.text: It says crowds are always criminal

hints.rationale: Emergent-norm theory rejects the 'inherently irrational' view, seeing crowds as developing their own norms.

hints.prompt: Emergent-norm theory does NOT view crowds as inherently irrational or deviant. Which two options describe crowds in exactly that negative way?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'irrational and impulsive' and 'prone to criminal behavior'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'able to develop their own definition of the situation' and 'often misinterpreted and misdirected'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'often misinterpreted and misdirected' and 'irrational and impulsive'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'prone to criminal behavior' and 'able to develop their own definition of the situation'

hints.rationale: Portraying crowds as irrational or criminal is exactly the negative view emergent-norm theory rejects.

questions.stem: According to emergent-norm theory, crowds are:

choices.label: C
choices.text: able to develop their own definition of the situation

questions.explanation: Emergent-norm theory holds that crowds develop their own norms and definition of the situation as members interact, rather than being inherently irrational.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 21 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 21 Q5 = C.

hints.prompt: This item concerns a theory that analyzes movements partly by their RESOURCES and their POSITIONS relative to other movements. That approach treats a movement somewhat like what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An organization competing for resources and position

hints.rationale: This approach treats a movement like an organization competing for resources and position.

hints.prompt: 'Framing' theory focuses on how movements DEFINE and present issues. How does that differ from analyzing a movement's resources and position among other movements?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Framing is about messaging and meaning, not resources and relative position

choices.label: B
choices.text: Framing is about budgets and staff

choices.label: D
choices.text: Framing is identical to resource analysis

hints.rationale: Framing concerns how movements present issues, unlike an analysis of resources and relative position.

hints.prompt: The item's theory analyzes movements by their resources and relative position. Which two options focus on something else, one on how issues are framed and the other on stages that build up to collective behavior?
hints.rationale: Framing concerns messaging and value-added theory concerns the build-up of conditions for collective behavior, not resources and relative position.

questions.stem: If we divide social movements according to their positions among all social movements in a society, we are using the __________ theory to understand social movements.
questions.explanation: Resource mobilization theory analyzes social movements in terms of their resources and their positions relative to other movements in society.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 21 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 21 Q7 = C.

hints.prompt: At its core, a 'social movement' involves many people acting together for what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A shared goal to change or establish norms, beliefs, or values

choices.label: C
choices.text: Random destruction for its own sake

hints.rationale: A social movement is many people acting together toward a shared goal to change norms, beliefs, or values.

hints.prompt: One option calls social movements 'disruptive and chaotic challenges to the government.' Are social movements defined by chaos and anti-government disruption?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No; they are organized collective efforts, not by definition chaotic or anti-government

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, chaos is what defines them

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, they must target the government

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because they are never organized

hints.rationale: Social movements are organized collective efforts, not inherently chaotic or anti-government.

hints.prompt: Which two options mischaracterize social movements, one as inherently chaotic and anti-government and the other as simply ineffective?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'disruptive and chaotic challenges to the government' and 'ineffective mass movements'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'the collective action of individuals working together to establish new norms, beliefs, or values' and 'the singular activities of a collection of groups'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'ineffective mass movements' and 'the collective action of individuals working together'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'the singular activities of a collection of groups' and 'disruptive and chaotic challenges to the government'

hints.rationale: Describing movements as inherently chaotic and anti-government or simply ineffective mischaracterizes them.

choices.label: A
choices.text: disruptive and chaotic challenges to the government

choices.label: C
choices.text: the collective action of individuals working together in an attempt to establish new norms beliefs, or values

choices.label: D
choices.text: the singular activities of a collection of groups working to challenge the status quo

questions.explanation: Social movements are organized collective actions in which people work together to establish new norms, beliefs, or values (or to resist change).
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 21 Section Quiz

questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 21 Q9 = C.

hints.prompt: In social-movement 'framing,' movements identify a problem, propose a solution, and motivate action. Specifying a SOLUTION to the problem is which of these functions?
hints.rationale: Specifying a remedy is the solution-proposing function of framing.

hints.prompt: 'Diagnostic' framing identifies the PROBLEM or its cause. How does that differ from stating 'outlawing cars' as the remedy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Diagnostic framing names the problem, whereas stating a remedy is the solution step

choices.label: C
choices.text: They are exactly the same step

hints.rationale: Diagnostic framing identifies the problem, unlike stating a remedy.

hints.prompt: 'Outlawing cars' is the proposed SOLUTION. Which two options describe other framing tasks, one urging people to act and the other changing how people fundamentally view an issue?
hints.rationale: Motivational framing urges action and frame transformation changes worldviews, neither of which is proposing the solution.

questions.stem: If a movement claims that the best way to reverse climate change is to reduce carbon emissions by outlawing privately owned cars, "outlawing cars" is the ________.
questions.explanation: Prognostic framing proposes a solution to the identified problem. Specifying 'outlawing cars' as the remedy is prognostic framing (diagnostic framing identifies the problem).
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 21 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 21 Q11 = A.

hints.prompt: 'Technology' as an agent of social change refers to tools, techniques, and innovations. Which of the following is a technological innovation?

choices.label: B
choices.text: The total number of people born

hints.rationale: Genetically engineered food is a technological innovation.

hints.prompt: Medical advances, the Internet, and genetically engineered food share what feature that population growth does not?

choices.label: D
choices.text: They are all unrelated to society

hints.rationale: Medical advances, the Internet, and GE food are technological innovations, whereas population growth is a demographic trend.

hints.prompt: This is a 'not' question. Which two options ARE clearly technologies, and so are not the exception?
hints.rationale: The Internet and genetically engineered food are clearly technological innovations.

questions.stem: When sociologists think about technology as an agent of social change, which of the following is not an example?
questions.explanation: Medical advances, the Internet, and genetically engineered food are technological agents of change; population growth is a demographic factor, not a technology.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 21 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 21 Q13 = A.

hints.prompt: When a powerful 'core' nation pushes a poorer 'peripheral' nation to modernize, there is a risk that the core nation might do what without care?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Impose its own values and harm the peripheral nation's interests

hints.rationale: Without care, a core nation may impose its values and harm the peripheral nation's interests.

hints.prompt: Which of the following is a genuine concern a core nation should keep in mind when promoting modernization elsewhere?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Preserving the peripheral nation's cultural identity

choices.label: B
choices.text: Maximizing its own hidden profit secretly

choices.label: D
choices.text: Speeding up regardless of any harm

hints.rationale: Preserving the peripheral nation's cultural identity is a genuine concern.

hints.prompt: You have confirmed that preserving cultural identity is a genuine concern. Before choosing only that, what should you verify?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Whether preparing for pitfalls and avoiding hegemonic assumptions are ALSO concerns

choices.label: B
choices.text: Whether it is the first option

choices.label: C
choices.text: Whether the option uses simpler words

choices.label: D
choices.text: Nothing; one real concern is always the complete answer

hints.rationale: Since several concerns all apply, check the other options before settling on one.

questions.stem: Core nations that work to propel peripheral nations toward modernization need to be aware of:

choices.label: B
choices.text: preparing for pitfalls that come with modernization

questions.explanation: Core nations promoting modernization must attend to cultural preservation, the pitfalls of modernization, and avoiding hegemonic assumptions, so all of the above applies.
